Sam: Welcome back to Cross-Show. Today we're talking about labor markets, and I'm joined by Skanda Amarnath, returning macro economist. Skanda Amarnath, thanks for being here.
Skanda Amarnath: Thanks, Sam. Great to be back.
Sam: Today's episode is sponsored by Bloomberg. Bloomberg Terminal is the data spine that institutional desks run on. Try bloomberg.com today.Before we get into it — Skanda Eminas was on episode 2 making this same point on Bloomberg-data caveats.
Skanda Amarnath: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Sam: I want to flag something — Skanda Eminas mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.
Skanda Amarnath: Two years ago I argued labor data was reliable at quarterly frequency. I now think only annual averages are trustworthy.
Sam: That's a real shift. Worth pulling on.Let's start there. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Skanda Amarnath: Yeah, so — Labor markets are where macro stops being abstract. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Sam: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Skanda Amarnath: Here's how I think about it. Risk is a household-level variable, not a portfolio-level one. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Sam: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.

Sam: I want to dig into that. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Amarnath: Sure. Risk is a household-level variable, not a portfolio-level one. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Sam: Let's pivot — systems thinking is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Skanda Amarnath: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around systems thinking cross-cut everything we've covered.
Sam: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do risk management and labor markets intersect?
Skanda Amarnath: Honestly, Labor markets are where macro stops being abstract. We've seen this play out at teams using BloombergNEF. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Sam: What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Amarnath: Yeah, so — Risk is a household-level variable, not a portfolio-level one. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

Skanda Amarnath: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
